content.text: SOC 2 was designed for software-as-a-service environments where the system boundary is well-defined and the processing steps are deterministic. Agentic AI systems break both assumptions: the system boundary now includes a third-party model provider, a vector database, and one or more external tools the agent is permitted to call; and the processing steps are non-deterministic by design. This document maps each SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (TSC) category to the specific controls, evidence artefacts, and operating cadences required when AI agents are in scope.

attrs.alt: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria mapping for agentic AI: CC6 logical access, CC7 system operations, CC9 risk management, PI processing integrity, C confidentiality, A+P availability and privacy, with AI-specific evidence artefacts spanning all categories

content.text: What It Is

SOC 2 control mapping is the process of identifying which specific controls, evidence artefacts, and operating procedures satisfy each SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (TSC) category. For traditional software systems, this mapping is well-understood: logical access controls satisfy CC6, monitoring satisfies CC7, and so on. For agentic AI systems, each TSC category requires AI-specific extensions because the standard software-centric controls do not fully address the risk surface introduced by non-deterministic processing, third-party model providers, and autonomous tool use.

The five TSC categories are: CC (Common Criteria, subdivided CC6 through CC9), PI (Processing Integrity), C (Confidentiality), A (Availability), and P (Privacy). CC is mandatory for all SOC 2 reports. PI, C, A, and P are optional add-on categories selected based on the commitments made to customers in service agreements.
Why It Matters

A SOC 2 Type II audit produces an opinion on whether controls were designed effectively and operated effectively throughout the audit period. The auditor does not invent the control framework — the organization defines its controls, and the auditor tests whether those controls are real and working. If an organization has not explicitly defined AI-specific controls and mapped them to TSC categories, the auditor will test the standard software controls and issue findings for gaps they observe in the AI system.

The practical consequence is that the mapping document — often called the System Description or the Control Environment — is the foundation of the entire audit. An incomplete mapping produces findings even when the underlying security posture is adequate, because the auditor cannot verify what was not described.
Benefits
Produces a single source of truth for what controls are in scope, who owns them, and what evidence demonstrates their operation.
Reveals gaps in control coverage before the auditor discovers them, enabling proactive remediation.
Reduces audit duration by providing auditors with a pre-mapped evidence library organized by TSC category and control ID.
Supports customer trust questionnaires (TQs) by providing accurate, consistent answers about security controls for AI systems.
Creates a reusable foundation for ISO 42001 mapping, since the AI-management-system controls in ISO 42001 align with the same risk categories.
Provides a defensible basis for risk acceptance decisions, because each accepted risk is linked to a specific TSC gap.
Trade-offs

The mapping document must be kept current. A control mapping that reflected the system design at the start of the audit period but was not updated when a new agent was deployed, a model provider was changed, or a RAG pipeline was added will produce findings when the auditor tests controls that no longer match the system description. Maintaining the mapping requires a change management trigger: any material change to the AI system should initiate a mapping review.

Overly broad mappings — listing many controls against a single TSC category without specificity — create audit difficulty. The auditor will test all listed controls and expect evidence for each. A mapping with ten controls where five are partially implemented is worse than a mapping with five controls that are fully implemented.
How It Works: The TSC Categories Applied to Agentic AI
CC6: Logical and Physical Access Controls

CC6 governs who and what can access the system and its data. For agentic AI systems, this extends beyond human user accounts to include agent service accounts and the tools those agents are permitted to call. Standard CC6 controls — multi-factor authentication, least-privilege access, quarterly access reviews — must be explicitly extended to cover agent identities.
Agent service account least-privilege.

Each agent's service account is scoped to the minimum permissions required for its function. A research agent that only needs to read from a knowledge base should not hold write permissions to any data store.
Tool permission matrix.
A documented matrix maps each agent role to the specific tools it is permitted to invoke and the scope of those tool permissions. This is the AI equivalent of a user role-permissions matrix.
Quarterly access review.

The access review process explicitly includes agent service accounts, not just human accounts. Each agent's permissions are reviewed against its current function and any excess permissions are revoked.
API key rotation.
API keys used by agents to call model providers and external tools are rotated on a defined schedule (typically 90 days) and immediately upon any suspected compromise.
CC7: System Operations

CC7 covers detection and response to security events and system anomalies. For AI systems, the primary CC7 controls are inference log retention and guardrail monitoring. Without complete inference logs, there is no way to reconstruct what an agent did during the audit period and no basis for anomaly detection.
Inference log retention.

Every agent inference is logged with: timestamp, agent ID, prompt version, input hash, output hash, tool calls made, and guardrail activation events. Logs are retained for a minimum of 90 days in tamper-resistant storage with SHA-256 manifests.
Guardrail activation monitoring.
Guardrail activation events — attempts by the agent to take an action outside its permitted scope — are logged, alerted on when above threshold, and reviewed in a weekly operations review.
Anomaly detection.
Statistical baselines for agent output patterns, tool call frequency, and response latency are monitored. Deviations beyond defined thresholds trigger alerts.
CC9: Risk Assessment and Vendor Management

CC9 governs how the organization identifies and manages risk, including risks from third-party vendors. For AI systems, the primary third-party risk is the model provider. If the model provider changes a model's behavior — through a model update, a fine-tune, or a policy change — the agent's outputs may change in ways that create new risks. CC9 controls require this vendor risk to be explicitly assessed and managed.
AI risk register.
A maintained register of AI-specific risks (hallucination, prompt injection, model drift, data leakage through context) with likelihood, impact, and mitigation status.
Vendor AI model risk assessment.
Each model provider is assessed for: SOC 2 certification status, data processing agreements, model versioning commitments, and notification processes for model changes.
Algorithmic impact assessment (AIA).

High-impact agentic AI systems undergo a documented AIA before deployment and when materially changed. The AIA assesses bias risk, error rate impact on affected populations, and the adequacy of human oversight.
PI: Processing Integrity

Processing integrity requires that the system processes data completely, accurately, timely, and as authorized. For non-deterministic AI systems, demonstrating processing integrity requires defining what "correct" processing means for the agent's function and then testing that definition operationally.
Prompt version pinning.
Every deployment of an agent records the exact prompt version in use. Inference logs reference this version, enabling reconstruction of the exact system prompt for any historical inference.
Input and output validation.
Input validation rejects malformed requests before they reach the model. Output validation checks that outputs conform to the expected schema and do not contain prohibited content categories.
Human-in-the-loop (HITL) for high-stakes decisions.
For agent actions above a defined impact threshold (financial transactions above a dollar limit, actions affecting external parties), a human approval step is required and the approval is logged.
C: Confidentiality

Confidentiality requires that information designated as confidential is protected. For AI systems, confidentiality controls must address system prompt secrecy, context window data that should not be logged, and RAG retrieval that must respect the user's data clearance level.
A + P: Availability and Privacy

Availability controls for AI systems must account for third-party model provider availability as an additional dependency. An SLO commitment that relies entirely on a single model provider API without a fallback mechanism is a design gap under the A category. Privacy controls must address data subject rights for data processed by the AI agent, including the right to know what data the agent used about an individual and the right to have that data deleted.
How to Implement: Building the Control Mapping
Step 1: Define the System Boundary

# soc2-system-boundary.yaml
# Defines what is in scope for the SOC 2 audit

system_name: "Meridian Agentic AI Platform"
audit_period: "2025-01-01 to 2025-12-31"
audit_type: "Type II"

in_scope_components:
  ai_agents:
    - name: research-agent
      model_provider: anthropic
      model_id: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
      tools_permitted: [web_search, knowledge_base_read]
      data_classification: internal
    - name: support-agent
      model_provider: openai
      model_id: gpt-4o
      tools_permitted: [crm_read, ticket_write]
      data_classification: confidential
  
  infrastructure:
    - cloud_provider: aws
      regions: [us-east-1, us-west-2]
      services: [eks, rds, s3, cloudwatch]
  
  third_party_vendors:
    - vendor: anthropic
      service: model_api
      soc2_certified: true
      dpa_in_place: true
    - vendor: openai
      service: model_api
      soc2_certified: true
      dpa_in_place: true

out_of_scope:
  - model_training (no training performed; API only)
  - physical_security (managed by AWS)
  - end_user_devices

tsc_categories_in_scope:
  - CC  # Common Criteria (mandatory)
  - PI  # Processing Integrity (committed in customer agreements)
  - C   # Confidentiality (committed in customer agreements)
  - A   # Availability (committed in SLAs)
  # P (Privacy) not in scope: no consumer PII processed
Step 2: Map Controls to TSC Sub-criteria

# generate_control_mapping.py
# Generates a control mapping document from a controls registry

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional


@dataclass
class Control:
    control_id: str
    tsc_category: str
    tsc_sub_criteria: str
    title: str
    description: str
    control_type: str  # preventive | detective | corrective
    control_owner: str
    operating_cadence: str
    evidence_artefacts: List[str]
    ai_specific: bool = False
    ai_artefacts: Optional[List[str]] = None


# AI-extended CC6 controls
cc6_agent_access = Control(
    control_id="CC6.AI-001",
    tsc_category="CC6",
    tsc_sub_criteria="CC6.3",
    title="Agent Service Account Least-Privilege",
    description=(
        "Agent service accounts are provisioned with the minimum permissions "
        "required to perform the agent's defined function. Permissions are "
        "defined in the tool permission matrix and reviewed quarterly."
    ),
    control_type="preventive",
    control_owner="security-eng@company.com",
    operating_cadence="quarterly + on change",
    evidence_artefacts=[
        "IAM policy document per agent service account",
        "Quarterly access review completion record",
        "Tool permission matrix (current version)",
    ],
    ai_specific=True,
    ai_artefacts=[
        "Tool permission matrix (YAML, version-controlled)",
        "Agent service account access review log",
    ],
)

# AI-extended CC7 controls
cc7_inference_logging = Control(
    control_id="CC7.AI-001",
    tsc_category="CC7",
    tsc_sub_criteria="CC7.2",
    title="Inference Log Retention and Integrity",
    description=(
        "All agent inferences are logged with tamper-resistant SHA-256 integrity "
        "proofs. Logs are retained for 90 days minimum in object-locked storage."
    ),
    control_type="detective",
    control_owner="platform-team@company.com",
    operating_cadence="continuous",
    evidence_artefacts=[
        "Inference log export (sampled dates)",
        "Log storage retention policy document",
        "SHA-256 manifest for sampled log files",
        "Access log showing compliance team query capability",
    ],
    ai_specific=True,
    ai_artefacts=[
        "Inference log schema (showing required fields)",
        "Guardrail activation report (monthly)",
        "Prompt version referenced in each log entry",
    ],
)


def generate_mapping_table(controls: List[Control]) -> str:
    rows = ["| Control ID | TSC | Title | Owner | Cadence | AI-Specific |"]
    rows.append("|---|---|---|---|---|---|")
    for c in controls:
        ai_flag = "Yes" if c.ai_specific else "No"
        rows.append(
            f"| {c.control_id} | {c.tsc_sub_criteria} | {c.title} "
            f"| {c.control_owner} | {c.operating_cadence} | {ai_flag} |"
        )
    return "\n".join(rows)


controls = [cc6_agent_access, cc7_inference_logging]
print(generate_mapping_table(controls))
Step 3: Validate Coverage with a Gap Analysis

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# validate-control-coverage.sh
# Check that all required TSC sub-criteria have at least one control mapped

set -euo pipefail

CONTROL_REGISTRY="${1:-controls.yaml}"
MISSING=0

# Required sub-criteria for an AI system with CC, PI, C, and A in scope
REQUIRED_SUBCRITERIA=(
  CC6.1 CC6.2 CC6.3 CC6.6 CC6.7 CC6.8
  CC7.1 CC7.2 CC7.3 CC7.4 CC7.5
  CC9.1 CC9.2
  PI1.1 PI1.2 PI1.3 PI1.4 PI1.5
  C1.1 C1.2
  A1.1 A1.2 A1.3
)

for sub_criteria in "${REQUIRED_SUBCRITERIA[@]}"; do
  count=$(grep -c "tsc_sub_criteria: ${sub_criteria}" "${CONTROL_REGISTRY}" 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
  if [[ "${count}" -eq 0 ]]; then
    echo "GAP: No control mapped to ${sub_criteria}"
    ((MISSING++)) || true
  fi
done

if [[ ${MISSING} -gt 0 ]]; then
  echo ""
  echo "${MISSING} TSC sub-criteria have no mapped controls. Address before audit."
  exit 1
else
  echo "All required TSC sub-criteria have at least one control mapped."
fi
AI-Specific Evidence Artefacts
The following artefacts are specific to agentic AI systems and span multiple TSC categories. Each must be included in the control mapping with a clear link to the controls they support.
Inference log export.

Supports CC7.2 (detection), C1.1 (data access auditing), and PI1.2 (processing completeness). Must include agent ID, timestamp, prompt version reference, input hash, output hash, tool calls, and guardrail activation events. Stored with SHA-256 manifest for integrity verification.
Guardrail activation report.
Supports CC7.3 (anomaly detection) and CC9.1 (risk monitoring). A monthly report showing guardrail activation counts by agent, rule, and severity. Spike detection alerts referenced in the report.
Prompt version history.

Supports PI1.1 (authorized processing) and CC6.8 (change management). A registry of all prompt versions, deployment dates, the engineer who approved deployment, and the change ticket number. Every production deployment must be linked to an entry in this registry.
Tool permission matrix.

Supports CC6.3 (least-privilege) and CC9.2 (vendor risk). Documents each agent's permitted tools, the scope of each tool permission (read vs. write, specific resources), and the approval that authorized each permission.
Model card.

Supports CC9.2 (vendor risk assessment) and C1.2 (data processing by third parties). Documents the model version in use, the provider's known limitations, the bias evaluation results, and the intended use case for which the model was assessed.
Algorithmic impact assessment.

Supports CC9.1 (risk assessment) and PI1.1 (authorized processing). A documented assessment of the potential for the agent's decisions to cause harm to individuals or groups, with mitigating controls and residual risk acceptance.
Real-World Example: Enterprise HR Agent

A B2B SaaS company deployed an agentic AI HR assistant that could answer employee questions about benefits, retrieve personal pay stubs, and initiate leave requests. During their first SOC 2 Type II audit, the auditor tested CC6.3 (logical access) and discovered that the agent's service account had read access to all employees' pay stubs, not just the records of the employee currently authenticated. This was a confidentiality finding (C1.1) and a logical access finding (CC6.3).

The root cause was that the data access layer applied user-scoping at the API request level but the agent service account held a broad read permission "for convenience" during development and this was never revoked. Had the organization built a tool permission matrix during the control mapping phase, they would have identified that the agent's service account permissions did not match its intended function (serving one user at a time) and remediated this pre-audit.
Common Mistakes
Mapping AI controls to TSC categories without AI-specific sub-criteria detail.
Stating "we monitor our AI system" maps to CC7 but does not satisfy the auditor without specifying what is monitored, at what frequency, and what the alert thresholds are.
Excluding agent service accounts from the access review.
The access review process covers human accounts by default. Agent service accounts must be explicitly added to the review scope.
Not updating the system description after model provider changes.
Switching from one model provider to another mid-audit-period without updating the system description creates an inconsistency between the described system and the actual system.
Claiming PI without determinism controls.

Processing integrity requires demonstrating that outputs are complete and accurate. For non-deterministic models, this requires defined test suites run against each prompt version before deployment, not just a general statement that the model is accurate.
No data processing agreement (DPA) with model providers.
CC9.2 requires vendor risk management for entities that process customer data on the organization's behalf. If the model provider receives customer data in the prompt, a DPA is required.
Treating guardrail configuration as static documentation.
Guardrail rules that are not version-controlled and change-managed cannot be evidenced to the auditor as having been consistently in place throughout the audit period.
Best Practices
Maintain the control mapping in a GRC platform with change tracking. Every update to the mapping should be versioned with a date, author, and reason for the change.
Organize the evidence library by TSC category and control ID, not by team or system. Auditors navigate by TSC category; organizing evidence by team creates unnecessary friction.
Add a change management trigger: any deployment of a new agent, any model provider change, and any update to guardrail rules should trigger a review of the relevant section of the control mapping.

For PI, define concrete acceptance criteria for what constitutes correct output for each agent's function. These criteria become the basis for pre-deployment testing and the evidence of processing integrity.

Reference the ISO 42001 AI management system standard alongside SOC 2. The two frameworks are complementary: SOC 2 tests controls; ISO 42001 certifies the management system that governs those controls. Organizations with both can use a unified control mapping.
Performance and Scalability

A control mapping for a single agentic AI system with CC, PI, C, and A in scope typically contains 35 to 60 individual controls. As additional agents are added to the system boundary, the control count grows primarily in CC6 (access controls are per-agent) and CC7 (monitoring is per-agent). The other categories scale more slowly because risk management (CC9), processing integrity (PI), and confidentiality (C) controls are often organization-wide rather than per-agent.

Evidence collection becomes the bottleneck at scale. Automate the packaging of inference log statistics, guardrail activation counts, and access review results into a structured evidence store that auditors can query directly. This reduces the time between the auditor's evidence request and the organization's response from days to hours.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Vanta and Drata.

Both platforms support custom controls with evidence linking. They automate evidence collection for standard cloud controls (AWS, GCP, Azure) and support manual evidence upload for AI-specific artefacts. Vanta's AI connector supports native integration with some model providers.
ServiceNow GRC.
The enterprise option. Supports complex control hierarchies, multi-framework mapping (SOC 2 + ISO 42001 + NIST AI RMF), and integration with ITSM for change management triggers.
AICPA SOC 2 guidance.

The AICPA's Points of Focus for each TSC criterion are the authoritative reference for what specific control attributes satisfy each criterion. The AI-specific control attributes are not yet explicitly enumerated in the AICPA guidance (as of mid-2025), but the Points of Focus framework extends cleanly to AI systems.
NIST AI RMF.

The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) maps to SOC 2 TSC categories with good coverage. Organizations using the NIST AI RMF as their internal AI governance framework can leverage that mapping to accelerate SOC 2 control definition.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability

The control mapping document and evidence library must themselves be governed under the controls they describe. The mapping document should be stored in a system with access controls (only the compliance team and named control owners can modify it), version history (every change is tracked), and availability sufficient to support the audit process. If the control mapping is stored in a system that goes down during the audit, the audit cannot proceed.

AI-specific evidence artefacts must not contain production PII. Inference logs that contain user inputs must be anonymized or pseudonymized before being included in the evidence library. The auditor needs to verify that logs exist and have the correct structure — they do not need to read the actual user content. Store log samples with PII fields hashed or redacted.
Mental Model

Think of the SOC 2 control mapping as the wiring diagram for your security posture. The TSC categories are the rooms in a building that must have specific safety systems — sprinklers, fire exits, smoke detectors. The control mapping document shows which specific systems are installed in which room, who is responsible for testing them, and how often they are tested. For an agentic AI system, several of those rooms (CC6, CC7, PI) need new types of safety systems that standard building codes do not yet specify — the mapping document is where you define them explicitly rather than waiting for an inspector to notice they are missing.
Key Takeaways

SOC 2 control mapping for agentic AI extends each TSC category with AI-specific controls: CC6 adds tool permission matrices and agent service account reviews; CC7 adds inference log retention and guardrail monitoring; CC9 adds AIA documentation and model vendor assessments.
The system description defines the audit boundary and must be kept current: any new agent, model provider change, or guardrail update triggers a mapping review.

Six AI-specific evidence artefacts span all TSC categories: inference log exports (with SHA-256 manifests), guardrail activation reports, prompt version history, tool permission matrices, model cards, and AIA documentation.
Processing integrity (PI) for non-deterministic AI requires concrete output acceptance criteria and per-deployment test results, not general accuracy claims.
Model providers who receive customer data in prompts require a data processing agreement (DPA) under CC9.2 vendor risk management.
ISO 42001 and NIST AI RMF are complementary to SOC 2: organizations using these frameworks can leverage unified control mappings that satisfy multiple standards simultaneously.
Evidence artefacts for AI controls must not contain production PII; use anonymized or hash-redacted log samples to demonstrate control operation without exposing user data.
